name: APF Medical & Nursing Volunteer Program
evidence: apfmed.org (English site) confirms placements are healthcare students/professionals volunteering directly inside Israeli hospital and clinic settings, but doesn't quantify the Israeli-to-overseas participant ratio.

name: Aardvark Israel
evidence: aardvarkisrael.com confirms an English-language, international 17-21 year-old population living together in Israeli cities with internships/ulpan, but does not detail how much daily mixing occurs with Israelis.

name: Achvat Amim
evidence: achvatamim.org (English site) describes participants "working directly with Palestinians and Israelis" and studying Hebrew/Arabic, indicating real but partial local engagement rather than full immersion.

name: Aish HaTorah – Aish Gesher
evidence: aish.com's homepage gave no program-specific detail; description indicates a traditional Old City yeshiva track for overseas young men from public-school backgrounds with no stated Israeli peer mixing.

name: Aish HaTorah – Essentials Program
evidence: The israel.aish.com/essentials fetch failed (certificate mismatch); relying on the description of a 28-day all-inclusive introductory program for ages 18-29 aimed at overseas newcomers to Judaism.

name: Aish HaTorah – Foundations Program
evidence: aish.com's homepage did not surface Foundations-specific detail; description depicts an overseas gap-year/semester-break audience studying Jewish texts with no mention of Israeli integration.

name: Alexander Muss High School in Israel (AMHSI)
evidence: amhsi.org (English site) confirms an American high-school study-abroad audience and college-prep framing, with no mention of Israeli student peers or Hebrew instruction.

name: Arava Institute for Environmental Studies
evidence: arava.org (English/Hebrew/Arabic site) confirms its mission of "preparing future leaders from Israel, Palestine, Jordan, and around the world" studying together, matching the description's cross-border cohort.

name: Arevim - Masa Year of Service
evidence: hholamit.org.il confirms participants live "like a local," volunteer 4 days/week inside Israeli hospitals and schools, and "exchange daily adventures with Israelis," a clear 40-80% mixed-integration pattern.

name: Ariel University International Program
evidence: ariel.ac.il/wp/en returned no usable content on this fetch; classification rests on the description's statement that students "live and study alongside Israeli peers" on the Ariel campus dorms.

name: Artzi
evidence: artzigap.org failed to resolve (DNS error); classification relies on the description's explicit statement that participants live alongside Israeli students from the Ruach Nachon academy, building Hebrew fluency and Israeli friendships.

name: Ateres Bnos Yerushalayim
evidence: No contactWebsite exists for this program; classification is based solely on the description of an English-oriented girls' seminary participating in Touro's Israel Option.

name: BBYO Passport: Israel Journey
evidence: bbyosummer.org redirects to bbyo.org/summer, a general BBYO teen-programs page; description confirms a guided 22-day teen tour (grades 8-12) that "engages with" but doesn't embed with Israeli communities.

name: BBYO Passport: Israel Trek
evidence: Same BBYO summer site as the Journey trip; description depicts an active touring/adventure itinerary (hiking, rafting, Gadna) rather than social integration with Israeli peers.

name: BINA Gap Year
evidence: bina.org.il/en confirms a program built around understanding "Israeli society from the inside" through communal living and volunteering, matching the description's Israeli-peer immersion.

name: Baer Miriam
evidence: baermiriam.com shows testimonials from Anglo students (e.g., Miami, FL) and MASA gap-year references, consistent with an English-speaking seminary population.

name: Bar-Ilan University-Yeshiva University Summer Science Research Internship
evidence: biu.ac.il was blocked by a bot-protection redirect; classification relies on the description that Yeshiva/Stern College undergrads are placed directly into Bar-Ilan University Israeli research labs.

name: Bat Ayin Yeshiva
evidence: batayin.org has been shut down by its host; classification relies on the description of an English-speaking Breslov-influenced yeshiva/smicha track for men roughly mid-20s to early-30s.

name: Beit Chana Seminary (Tzfat) – Chul Program
evidence: beitchana.org is primarily Hebrew and lists the Chul (overseas) program as a distinct registration track from the Israeli seminar track, but requires strong Hebrew proficiency and a shared Limudei Kodesh curriculum.

name: Bezalel Academy of Arts and Design - Student Exchange Program
evidence: bezalel.ac.il/en offers Hebrew/English/Arabic toggles and lists an incoming student-exchange track; combined with the description's Hebrew-or-English coursework and Hebrew University dorm housing, this points to real campus mixing with Israeli students.

name: Big Idea Gap Year
evidence: aardvarkisrael.com/big-idea confirmed the parent org's 17-21 age range but had no Big Idea-specific detail; description's internship placement at real Be'er Sheva tech companies (Wix, PayPal) alongside Hebrew study supports medium integration.

name: Birthright Israel
evidence: No contactWebsite was provided; classification relies on the well-documented Birthright format (ages 18-32) which typically includes an Israeli-peer "mifgash" segment alongside the touring itinerary.

name: Birthright Israel Excel Fellowship
evidence: birthrightisraelexcel.com redirected to taglitisrael.co.il/excel (not re-fetched); description of a competitive 10-week Tel Aviv internship at Israeli startups/firms for high-achieving Jewish college students supports workplace-level integration.

name: Bnei Akiva Olamit: Shalem
evidence: worldbneiakiva.org's homepage (English/Hebrew mix) did not contain Shalem-specific content; classification relies on the description of national-service placements at Israeli institutions (Beit Meir Hospital, ALUT) for overseas girls.

name: Bnei David (Eli)
evidence: bneidavid.org is entirely in Hebrew and serves as a mainstream Israeli religious-Zionist mechina/yeshiva network; the description confirms overseas students simply integrate into the standard, overwhelmingly Israeli, pre-army programs.

name: Bnos Chava Seminary
evidence: Only a Facebook page is listed as contactWebsite (not fetched); classification is based on the description of a one-year teacher-training program for recent Bais Yaakov (Anglo) high school graduates.

name: Bnos Sarah Seminary
evidence: masaisrael.org/program/bnos-sarah confirms the seminary "exclusively serves English-speaking overseas students" from North America/Europe, ages 17-19, despite 80% of classes being taught in Hebrew.

name: Buchmann-Mehta School of Music International Program
evidence: en-arts.tau.ac.il/Music/Inter-Prog confirms an international student body (30+ countries) at Bachelor's/Master's/Artist Diploma levels studying at Tel Aviv University under Israeli (IPO) mentors and in bilingual Hebrew/English productions.

name: CAMERA Fellows - Student Leadership and Advocacy Mission to Israel
evidence: cameraoncampus.org confirms a college-student fellowship/advocacy focus across 80+ campuses; description shows a guided two-week lecture-and-touring mission rather than social integration with Israelis.

name: CTeen Summer: Heritage Quest (Poland-Israel)
evidence: cteensummer.com confirms an American-teen customer base (testimonials from US cities) on a guided Poland-Israel heritage trip, with no mention of Israeli peer interaction.

name: Career Israel
evidence: careerisrael.com confirms interns are placed directly at real Israeli companies (WalkMe, Strauss, Mobile ODT, BDO Ziv Haft) while living in a "tight-knit" international young-professional community.

name: Chai Israel
evidence: chaiisrael.com failed to resolve (DNS error); classification relies on the description of a 17-19 year-old program combining classes, volunteering, and touring with no stated Israeli-peer mixing.

name: Chasedei Breslov Yeshiva
evidence: breslov.com/Yeshiva (English site) advertises an "International Remote Study Program," consistent with the description's English-speaking kollel/yeshiva serving overseas students since 1987.

name: Chemdas Bais Yaakov
evidence: No contactWebsite exists; classification is based solely on the description of a Jerusalem seminary in Touro's Israel Option, a track historically serving Anglo students.

name: Chevron Yeshiva (Knesset Yisrael)
evidence: chevronyeshiva.org is a placeholder "under construction" page with minimal detail, but the description of a ~1,400-student, predominantly Israeli-student, Hebrew-speaking Litvish yeshiva with no formal overseas track strongly supports high integration.

name: Conservative Yeshiva
evidence: fuchsbergcenter.org confirms a Jerusalem beit midrash "welcoming leaders and seekers from around the world" of "all ages," from school/college-age through older adults, matching the description's egalitarian, all-backgrounds Masorti framing.

name: Darchei Binah
evidence: darcheibinah.org's homepage gave no demographic detail; classification relies on the description of a women's seminary offering a gap year plus an optional accredited second year with secular college coursework via Touro/Daemen partnerships.

name: Diaspora Yeshiva – Gap Year Program
evidence: diasporayeshiva.com confirms a Mount Zion yeshiva for male high-school graduates with a Yeshiva-B.A. gap-year track, serving an English-speaking overseas population with no stated Israeli-peer mixing.

name: Diller Teen Fellows Israel Summer Seminar
evidence: dillerteenfellows.org confirms Israeli teens from cities like Ashkelon, Be'er Sheva, Haifa, and Tel Aviv are full partner-community fellows alongside diaspora teens at the Global Diller Congress, not just background locals.

name: Dvar Yerushalayim
evidence: dvar.org.il confirms it serves "Jews from all over the world" with a Beginners Program and Hebrew Ulpan aimed at those with minimal prior background, consistent with an English-speaking overseas population.

name: ELI Gap Year Program
evidence: TAU's ELI page states it is for 'recent high school graduates from any country' with English-taught coursework and mentorship from Israeli entrepreneurs, indicating a diaspora cohort with meaningful but partial contact with Israeli academic/business figures.

name: EYAHT - Aish HaTorah College for Women
evidence: Aish.com's homepage gave no program-specific detail, but the description targets unaffiliated, college-educated English-speaking women ages 22-30 exploring observance, an overwhelmingly non-Israeli English-speaking cohort.

name: Eco-Israel Program
evidence: The website (ecoisrael.org.il) failed to resolve in multiple attempts; the description describes an English-speaking 18-24 cohort on a permaculture farm with no explicit Israeli-student mixing mentioned.

name: Ein Prat Academy for Leadership
evidence: The site returned a 403 error, but the description explicitly confirms the academy is 'primarily Israeli-only and Hebrew-language' for Israeli high-school graduates before IDF service.

name: Emunah College - Faculty of Arts and Design
evidence: The college's main site (emuna.emef.ac.il) is in Hebrew and describes preparing 'leading female creators in Israel,' indicating an Israeli-majority institution that the described English-support track for overseas women integrates into for an accredited Israeli degree.

name: En-Gedi Oasis Archaeological Project
evidence: The dig's page describes participants generically with a stated minimum age of 16 and Hebrew University credit available, without clarifying nationality mix, so the existing medium tag typical of similar HUJI-run field schools was retained.

name: FZY Year Course
evidence: FZY's site describes serving '12-25 year olds' and 'university students' across UK and international programs, consistent with a mixed Anglo-cohort/Israeli-engagement gap year split between Jerusalem and Tel Aviv.

name: Garin Tzabar
evidence: Garin Tzabar's site describes bringing overseas young Jewish adults to 'make Aliyah...and serve...in the IDF as Lone Soldiers,' meaning participants are fully embedded day-to-day in Israeli military units and host kibbutzim despite the cohort itself being overseas-recruited.

name: Ginsburg-Ingerman Overseas Student Program (OSP)
evidence: BGU's study-abroad site explicitly promotes 'opportunities to connect with international and Israeli students' and describes Israeli dorm suitemates alongside its English-taught semester/year coursework.

name: HUC-JIR Year-in-Israel
evidence: HUC's site confirms a mandatory graduate Year-in-Israel for rabbinical/cantorial/Jewish-education students but does not describe mixed classes with Israeli students, matching a mostly-diaspora-cohort program with community-service-level local contact.

name: Habonim Dror Australia - Shnat
evidence: The Shnat page refers to a 'Habonim Dror Shnat Hachshara Anglo' program for overseas English-speakers doing kibbutz and Jerusalem-based civil-society work, consistent with its sibling Shnat programs' medium-integration rating.

name: Habonim Dror Southern Africa - Shnat
evidence: The (redirected) Habonim Dror Southern Africa site describes the movement broadly for 'Jewish youth ages 9-24' without Shnat-specific detail, so the description's kibbutz-and-Jerusalem-training format was used to keep the medium rating consistent with sibling programs.

name: Habonim Dror UK - Shnat
evidence: The UK site explicitly invites those 'finishing sixth form/college' to the 'Shnat Israel programme,' combining kibbutz time with volunteering, teaching English, and communal living in an Israeli city.

name: Habonim Dror Workshop
evidence: The North American site labels its offering 'For High School Graduates...A nine-month gap-year program...volunteering...and engagement with Israeli society,' including volunteering with an Israeli youth movement.

name: Hadassah Medical Center Volunteer Program
evidence: Hadassah International's general site had no volunteer-program section; the description states volunteers are placed 'alongside nursing staff' in Israeli hospitals, indicating real if staff-mediated daily contact with Israelis.

name: Hasbara Fellowships Israel Training Program
evidence: The site describes bringing 'hundreds of students' from 'campuses throughout North America' (plus a High School Internship track) for meetings with Israeli figures, a short mission-style program rather than sustained daily mixing.

name: Hashomer Hatzair Shnat Hachshara
evidence: The org's page describes a diaspora movement 'present in dozens of countries'; the program description states participants live communally in Israel 'becoming part of Israeli society,' matching its Habonim-family peers' medium rating.

name: Hazor Excavations
evidence: The dig's site gave only a registration-open notice with no participant demographics, so the description's Kibbutz Kfar HaNassi field-school format (typical of similar HUJI-run digs) was used to assign medium integration.

name: Heichal Yerushalayim
evidence: The site's tagline, 'Sephardic Community Yeshiva in Israel for Post High School Students,' targets overseas English-speaking students 'returning home to various communities,' indicating minimal local mixing.

name: Hevruta Gap-Year Program
evidence: Both the Hartman Institute site and the description confirm the program is split '70...half from North America, half from Israel,' a near-even Anglo/Israeli mix.

name: Hippos-Sussita Excavations
evidence: The dig's site gave no participant demographics; medium integration was assigned by analogy to similar Israeli-university-run field schools at kibbutz-adjacent excavation sites.

name: Interuniversity Institute for Marine Sciences (IUI) Eilat - Intensive Courses
evidence: IUI's site explicitly invites 'students and researchers from around the world' while being 'open to all students from higher education institutions in Israel,' confirming a real Israeli/international mix in its courses.

name: Israel Free Spirit
evidence: The site describes Jewish young adults ages 18-32 departing from North American cities on a Birthright-style trip; the classic Birthright format and this program's Yachad/Yalla partnership typically include an Israeli-peer component.

name: Israel Government Fellows (IGF)
evidence: The Begin Center site states IGF 'is the only existing program of its kind for non-Israelis,' with fellows working daily inside Israeli government ministries/NGOs alongside Israeli officials and in weekly seminars with policymakers.

name: Israel Lacrosse Gap Year Program
evidence: The Israel Lacrosse site states teams are 'comprised of native Israeli and North American based players,' a direct on-field mix of Israelis and overseas participants.

name: Israel Tech Challenge Fellows Program
evidence: The Masa tech-challenge URL and a follow-up search of masaisrael.org both failed to surface program specifics; the description's 10-month training with Israeli and multinational companies was used to keep the existing medium rating.

name: Israel XP (Bar-Ilan University Overseas Student Program)
evidence: Bar-Ilan's Israel XP page describes the program for 'overseas students' and 'Jewish youth from abroad' in English-taught courses, without describing daily mixing with Israeli students.

name: Isralight
evidence: Isralight's site gives only a general worldwide-Jewish-renaissance mission statement with no participant demographics; the description's short, flexible-length, English-language Old City learning format supported a low-integration, all-ages assignment.

name: JEWEL for Women
evidence: No contactWebsite was available; the description states the program is 'taught in English with no Hebrew required' for ages 19-31, a hallmark of a low-integration English-speaking bubble.

name: JTS Israel Year
evidence: JTS's Israel page describes a required year for rabbinical/graduate students 'to engage with Israeli society, history and politics' at the Israeli Schechter Institute, indicating meaningful but institution-mediated local engagement.

name: Jerusalem Academy of Music and Dance – DanceJerusalem
evidence: JAMD's site references 'international students' financial aid within its Bachelor's/Master's dance degree; as a mainstream Israeli conservatory, technical dance training is presumed to occur alongside Israeli majors even though the Rothberg Israel-studies coursework is international-only.

name: Jerusalem College of Technology - International English Speakers Program
evidence: JCT's page names it the 'International English Speakers Program,' with Torah study and a CS/Business degree both delivered in English mornings/afternoons, run separately from the college's Hebrew-track Israeli students.

name: Kadima (World Bnei Akiva)
evidence: World Bnei Akiva's general site did not detail Kadima specifically; the description's internships, kibbutz living, Magen David Adom training and a taste of Marva IDF prep imply real day-to-day contact with Israeli institutions and staff.

name: Kibbutz Lotan Green Apprenticeship
evidence: Kibbutzlotan.com (in Hebrew) describes the kibbutz as home to new immigrants, Israeli soldiers, and 'volunteers arriving from around the world,' suggesting the apprenticeship has genuine contact with the kibbutz's Israeli membership, though the fetched page was general rather than apprenticeship-specific.

name: Kibbutz Program Center (KPC)
evidence: KPC's site explicitly promises volunteers the chance to 'live and work with both Israeli youngsters as well as with other kibbutz volunteers,' a direct mixed-population claim.

name: Kibbutz Ulpan
evidence: The Kibbutz Ulpan site states 'Olim live alongside tourists' and describes 'living and working alongside locals on a kibbutz' while studying Hebrew, confirming real day-to-day contact with Israelis.

name: Kivunim
evidence: Kivunim's site confirms a high-school-graduate gap year with heavy multi-country travel; consistent with the description's academic/travel focus rather than deep local-Israeli mixing, the existing low rating was kept.

name: Kol Ami Mechina
evidence: Kol Ami's site states the program is for 'ISRAELI & INTERNATIONAL HIGH SCHOOL GRADUATES' who 'CONNECT' for a 6-9 month gap year, confirming a direct, roughly balanced Israeli/overseas mix.

name: Leket Israel Volunteer Program
evidence: Leket's site returned a 403 error; the description describes flexible, no-experience volunteer sessions (gleaning, food rescue, sandwich-packing) without specifying an Israeli-participant mix, so a low rating was assigned with low confidence.

name: Limmud (World Bnei Akiva)
evidence: World Bnei Akiva's general site did not mention the Limmud track specifically; the description's focus on internal Torah study and personal-growth programming, without kibbutz/internship/MDA components like its sibling Kadima, supported keeping the existing low integration rating.

name: Livnot U'Lehibanot
evidence: No contactWebsite was available; description describes an English-speaking Jewish-identity/volunteering program in Tzfat and Jerusalem with no mention of Israeli co-participants, so classified from description alone.

name: MDA Overseas Volunteers Program
evidence: Live site (afmda.org) confirms international volunteers ages 18-40 work real ambulance shifts alongside Israeli paramedics/EMTs, a meaningful but not full immersion into Israeli society.

name: MMY (Michlelet Mevaseret Yerushalayim)
evidence: Live site (mevaseret.org) is in English, targets North American post-high-school women (alumnae from Teaneck, Queens, etc.) with no mention of Israeli student integration.

name: MTA (World Bnei Akiva)
evidence: Description states MTA is geared specifically to Anglo (Australia/NZ/South Africa) participants for immersive Beit Midrash learning, with no stated Israeli-participant mixing; worldbneiakiva.org homepage fetch did not surface MTA-specific details.

name: Maccabi Gap Year
evidence: Website fetch was blocked (403); description's kibbutz living/work, taste of IDF Marva training, and MDA EMS training imply regular contact with Israelis even though the program itself is Anglo/movement-run.

name: Mach Hach BaAretz
evidence: Live site (machhachbaaretz.org) confirms an English-language summer tour for '10th-grade graduates' with 600+ participants each summer from the Bnei Akiva movement, with no described Israeli-participant mixing.

name: Machaneh Bonim B'Israel (MBI)
evidence: Live site (hdnaisrael.org) confirms MBI is an English-language program 'for rising 11th graders' drawing from Habonim Dror North America camps; 'engagement with Israeli society' is touring-based, not co-living/studying with Israeli peers.

name: Machon Alte - Seminary Chaya Mushka
evidence: Live site (machonalte.com) is in English, markets to women 'from 6 continents' ages 18-29 (plus a 45-65 track) and mentions some Israeli participants, but the overall framing and testimonials (Binghamton, Montreal, Florida) are overwhelmingly Anglo/international.

name: Machon Maayan
evidence: Live site (machonmaayan.org) is in English for gap-year women, and description notes Israeli internships and a shared Israeli campus (gym/pool/track), indicating regular contact with Israeli society beyond a purely Anglo bubble.

name: Machon Meir
evidence: Live site (machonmeir.net) explicitly says 'Learn in English in an authentic Israeli yeshiva' serving 'students from around the world,' with no stated Israeli-student integration beyond a Hebrew ulpan component.

name: Machon Raaya
evidence: No contactWebsite was available; description identifies it as a Har Nof seminary participating in Touro/Hebrew Theological College's Israel Option programs, i.e. an Anglo post-high-school women's seminary track.

name: Machon Roni
evidence: Diaspora Yeshiva site fetch was in English (with Hebrew/Yiddish phrases) but had no Machon Roni-specific detail; description describes English- and Spanish-language tracks for conversion candidates with no Israeli-participant mixing described.

name: Machon Sara Yerushalayim (MSY)
evidence: Live site (machonsara.org) confirms an international, English/Hebrew-mixed seminary with a NY recruiting office, consistent with a Bais Yaakov-track Anglo post-high-school women's program.

name: Machon Shlomo
evidence: Live site (machonshlomo.org) states verbatim: 'We serve Jewish, English-speaking college graduates in their 20s and 30s' pausing careers for Torah study, with no mention of Israeli participants.

name: Machon Yaakov
evidence: Live site (machonyaakov.org) is in English with Anglo testimonials (Ezra G., Denes B., Mike R.); description confirms a highly selective Har Nof yeshiva for ages 21-30.

name: Masa Israel Teaching Fellows (MITF)
evidence: Live site (masaisrael.org) is in English and includes an alumnus testimonial noting it is 'one of the only programs where you actually live with Israelis,' corroborating placement as English-teaching assistants in Israeli public schools.

name: Mayanot Post-High-School Program (Men)
evidence: Live site (mayanot.edu) confirms in English that the 'Post High School Learning Program targets 18-19 year olds' from 'diverse Jewish backgrounds' with no mention of Israeli-participant integration.

name: Mayanot Women's Program
evidence: Live site (mayanot.edu) is in English, describing 'women from all over the world'; description gives explicit ages 20-29 for this Judaic-studies track with no Israeli-participant mixing noted.

name: Mechina Olamit (World Bnei Akiva)
evidence: Description explicitly states 'shared living with Israeli peers' at a co-ed international mechina campus; worldbneiakiva.org homepage fetch did not surface program-specific corroboration.

name: Mechinat Aderet
evidence: mechinot.org.il/en homepage fetch confirms the JCM network recruits students from other countries into these Hebrew-based Israeli mechinot alongside the primarily-Israeli cohort, consistent with a mixed population.

name: Mechinat Amichai
evidence: Same JCM network as other mechinot (mechinot.org.il/en) which recruits some overseas students into a primarily-Israeli Hebrew-speaking mechina cohort.

name: Mechinat Arava
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting overseas students into a mixed cohort deliberately open to all Jewish denominations.

name: Mechinat Be'eri
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort.

name: Mechinat Beit Yisrael (Big)
evidence: Live site (reut.org.il) confirms about 70 Israeli high-school graduates from across the country attend, with no international track mentioned, matching the description's 'Israeli-only, Hebrew-language program with no overseas track.'

name: Mechinat Bnei David (Eli)
evidence: Live site (bneidavid.org) is entirely in Hebrew and Israeli-army-focused, confirming the description's 'Israeli-only, Hebrew-language program with no overseas track.'

name: Mechinat Dror
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort in the western Negev.

name: Mechinat Five Fingers (Chamesh Etzbaot)
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort.

name: Mechinat HaEmek
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en); description explicitly emphasizes 'building bridges across different sectors of Israeli society,' consistent with a mixed cohort.

name: Mechinat Hannaton
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort in the Yizrael Valley.

name: Mechinat Haviva Reich
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort preparing for IDF service.

name: Mechinat Lachish
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort.

name: Mechinat Meitzar
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort on the Golan Heights.

name: Mechinat Melach HaAretz
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort across its two single-gender Dead Sea campuses.

name: Mechinat Nachshon
evidence: Website fetch failed (connection reset); description explicitly states it 'runs in Hebrew and is primarily for Israelis,' with overseas participants only joining in small numbers through Yachad.

name: Mechinat Rabin
evidence: Website fetch failed (DNS error); description explicitly states it 'has a dedicated international track welcoming non-Israeli participants ages 18-19 to study alongside Israeli peers,' indicating a genuine mixed cohort.

name: Mechinat Rakia
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort in Harish.

name: Mechinat Tavor
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en) recruiting some overseas students into a mixed, primarily-Israeli mechina cohort spanning northern Israel communities.

name: Mechinat Telem
evidence: Same JCM network (mechinot.org.il/en); description emphasizes coexistence work within Tel Aviv-Jaffa's diverse Jewish and Arab communities, consistent with a mixed cohort.

name: Medical School for International Health (MSIH)
evidence: Website fetch failed (DNS error); description confirms an English-taught, North American-style M.D. program requiring a prior bachelor's degree and MCAT/GAMSAT, with Medical Hebrew study but a fourth year completed at Columbia, indicating limited but real exposure to Israeli campus life.

name: Meorot Yerushalayim
evidence: Live site (gomeorot.com) is in English and explicitly frames itself as a gap-year seminary ('Before college - you need college for your soul'), for women from public school/non-observant backgrounds, with no Israeli-participant integration mentioned.

name: Michlalah Jerusalem College – Machal (Overseas Students Program)
evidence: Live site (michlalahmachal.com) calls it 'The Linda Pinsky School for Overseas Students' with students from '30 high schools world-wide' and no mention of Israeli peer integration.

name: Midreshet AMIT
evidence: Site (midreshetamit.org) is English-language with North American testimonials and no stated Israeli-student integration; chesed work is with Israeli children, not peer students, matching the typical anglo-seminary pattern.

name: Midreshet Amudim
evidence: Site (amudimisrael.org) explicitly describes it as 'An Israel Gap Year Seminary Program for Post High School Modern Orthodox Jewish Women' with no mention of Israeli students or local integration.

name: Midreshet B'erot Bat Ayin
evidence: Site (berotbatayin.org) is English-only, welcomes 'women of all ages and nationality,' with no stated Israeli-student mixing; broad age range covers gap-year through older baalot teshuva/conversion candidates.

name: Midreshet Ein Hanatziv
evidence: Description states overseas students are 'fully immersed with 60-80 Israeli students living on the same schedule' on a working kibbutz, indicating a genuine anglo/Israeli mix rather than a segregated overseas bubble.

name: Midreshet Eshel
evidence: English-language site (sephardicseminary.org) gives no explicit population breakdown; description and legacy tag both point to a typical anglo overseas seminary near the Kotel.

name: Midreshet HaRova
evidence: Website fetch was blocked (403); description's emphasis on 'overseas program' with a focus on Aliyah/connection to Israel, not on Israeli peers, matches the existing low-integration tag.

name: Midreshet Lindenbaum
evidence: Site (midreshet-lindenbaum.org.il) lists 'Israeli Integration' as a named program feature alongside a separate Hebrew-language 'Israeli programs' section, suggesting a real (if partial) mix beyond a purely overseas track.

name: Midreshet Lindenbaum
evidence: Same institution/site as Midreshet Lindenbaum above, which advertises 'Israeli Integration' as a program feature alongside its overseas tracks.

name: Midreshet Moriah
evidence: Site (midreshetmoriah.org) testimonials are exclusively from North American cities (Chicago, Brooklyn, London, etc.) with no mention of Israeli peers.

name: Midreshet Rachel v'Chaya
evidence: Description explicitly targets 'English-speaking Orthodox women in their 20s and 30s (college graduates and professionals)'; site (darchenoam.org) gives no indication of Israeli-student integration.

name: Midreshet Tehillah
evidence: Site (midreshettehillah.com) references 'Shana Alef/Bet' and a gap-year recruitment page for 'recent high school graduates' with no stated Israeli-student mixing.

name: Midreshet Torat Chessed
evidence: Site (toratchessed.com) testimonials are all from American day schools (Yavneh Manchester, HAFTR NY, Maayanot NJ, etc.) with no mention of Israeli students; chesed work is with at-risk children, not integration with Israeli peers.

name: Migdal Oz (SKA Beit Midrash for Women)
evidence: Site (skamigdaloz.org) pairs 'an authentic Israeli experience and a friendly English-speaking peer group,' with a testimonial saying 'I have so many Israeli friends and I'm surrounded by Israelis all the time' and learning 'in Hebrew with Israelis,' matching the description's ~130 fully-integrated overseas-and-Israeli shana aleph cohort.

name: Mir Yeshiva (Yeshivas Mir Yerushalayim)
evidence: Site (themir.org) confirms students from 28 countries but does not describe daily mixing between the large American/overseas division and the predominantly Israeli/Haredi core beit midrash, consistent with the description's framing of a separate overseas division for English speakers.

name: Musrara International Residency Program
evidence: Description explicitly states the juried residency selects 'five interdisciplinary artists from Israel and abroad,' a direct mix of Israeli and overseas participants working alongside each other in Jerusalem.

name: NCSY GIVE (Girls Israel Volunteer Experience)
evidence: Website (give.ncsy.org / ncsy.org) gave no population details; description's volunteering (soup kitchens, kibbutz work) is service-oriented touring for American teens rather than sustained integration with Israeli peers.

name: NCSY Kollel
evidence: Site (kollel.ncsy.org) gave no population breakdown; description centers on Torah learning, sports leagues, and touring among the American teen cohort with no mention of Israeli integration.

name: NCSY Next Step (Israel Internships)

name: Nativ College Leadership Program
evidence: No contactWebsite available; description's inclusion of army service (Marva) alongside Israel study suggests meaningful contact with Israeli soldiers/society, though this is inferred from text alone.

name: Nesiya Kehillah Summer Program
evidence: The nesiya.org domain now appears to be defunct/parked (fetch redirected to an unrelated site), so classification relies on the description's explicit statement that 'Israeli and North American participants travel together.'

evidence: Parent site (nevey.org) targets 'Jewish women 20 to 33 years old' seeking foundational Jewish knowledge (baalot teshuva focus), with no stated Israeli-student mixing.
